speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Gus Demus took place at his home in Potlatch on August 7, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schreger.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I wanted to ask you about where you grew up. You know, in Greece, what it was like where you grew up.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, if I tell you the truth, there were certain conditions. 50 years ago, now, there are a little bit better, a little better. But when I grew up, it was a village. Oh, 300 people. Maybe more, I don't know. Then you have nothing there, no convenience of any kind. There were no warring houses. Everything was, you had to pull the water from well, you know, to use it for houses, for everything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know if they were conditioner, pretty bad there. But they lived through, and they're still bad now. You see, I was over there about 10, 12 years ago, and they haven't changed much. The only thing they have there is transportation. You see, the United States went over there and built a lot of roads. They have the transportation now, but they don't have very much other convenience, no electricity,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: no water, as I said. There's no boring house, no electricity. They had to light themselves with lamps. They have lamps now. At the time when I grew up, they had oil, olive oil, and, well, they had a pot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the river, they put the oil, and then they light the, I don't know what you call that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Weak.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The light, the weak, you see, and that's where they, but now they have what they call the gas. You see, they're pumping. They have them, gas lamps. Not everybody, but those that are eating stores and things like that, during private hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the farming there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Mostly farming, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The terrain is barren, you see. They have a little bit of land in the flood. There's a flood all about, I don't know how many miles. And that's why they raise their wheat and everything. They used to raise potatoes, and there is the best potatoes I ever tested. They got the seed from Germany. There is good potatoes. That's one good thing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But all the things, now they have probably some of those that they didn't have much land. They have to dig,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: well, you know,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: sides of a mountain, you see, you know what I mean?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The mountainside, they have to dig and find water, what I find in north, you see, and kind of dammit there with the rocks. And they used up the well for a garden or anything, wheat or anything, but they didn't have no water, you see. And of course, it grew up, there was much rain. But now they have different systems, because they are well enough to buy the gas pump to pump the water to irrigate the land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you grew up there, was there much future for you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There wasn't any future of any kind.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When I grew up, when I left the school, oh, about a year, I guess, after I left the school, I had different jobs. And my father sent me to an island where he had a cousin, you know, and he had a, a Becky, he was baking bread. And I worked over there for three years before I came to this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far was that from home?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That was about 15 miles. And you couldn't, you couldn't leave there, you see. It was an island and you had to have a boat or something to cross the water. Because in other places where he sent me, I was just kidding, I'm seeking a home. I think, so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked in the bakery, did you make money? Did they give you money?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, the first few years, the first two years, I didn't get any money, they're family. He used to give me flour. When they were short of flour, you see, there's nothing in the land. Well, he used to give me flour. But after that, I drew salary and gave me so much money. And I had to eat from that and do everything else. Although I had free laundry and free clothing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I'm sure it was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But he did give me so much money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the flour go to the family, to your family?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, at the time, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how old were you when you started working for him? How old?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I see, I say, there's three years, I'm 17 years, about 13, 14 years old. Because I work in different places, in town there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I get how sick I can go home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you make school?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: School for years. I had four years in school. Six and four is ten years.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Ten years old when I left the school. And then I worked there for years in different places. Sometimes it didn't work. I just stayed home.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And finally they sent me over to this island. I couldn't get away from that, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't like it too much, wasn't it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I didn't like it very well, but I got used to it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It wasn't very pleasant at first, but then I got used to that. I liked it too much, it was better. Then I was grown, you know, it was better than going there at home and had nothing to look for, you see. Nothing to eat, even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many kids in the family with you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Seven.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Five boys and two girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it that was bringing people to America? Did they hear much about America over there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, sure they heard much about America.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When I left there, they had been here in America for probably four, five, six, seven, eight years. They heard about America, but they couldn't get enough money to buy the ticket to leave there. Otherwise they all were here, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they heard about America, yes, they sure they heard. They thought that America was a good country, and it is still a good country. They come here with the intention to make money and go back. They lived there, you see, and a lot of them did. But I, myself, I never had the... After I learned how to work here and how to live,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I didn't want to go back. From here, my cousins, some of my cousins went back and got married. There is a family, and of course they had a few dollars from here, you see. But then after that way, some succeeded and some fell. Some bought land, you see, with the money they brought from here. And then it wasn't even good. But others, others didn't.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Each time you see in America, some succeed and others fail.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not everybody did the riches, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of what people heard in Greece that America was a land of opportunity?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, sure.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they came with the purpose to get the money. But of course, a lot of them did, a lot of them stayed here, you see. There's many Greeks here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They're in a good position to... Some of you know Pandasius, who had the theaters.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a Greek.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Another fellow in theater business, schoolers. They made big money, he got rich.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But Pandasius, of course, he went on the...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know what happened to him last day, lost everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people know that there was going to be a lot of work available when they got here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, a lot of fellas in the Greeks that were here, you know. They were...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They were...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They found more.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They found more.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Larry knew, called a lot of fellas, you know, that they were here before those. And they knew the language. They could speak the language.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They took advantage of them, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They made them pay money for the jobs, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean like these contractors who had hired?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, but they were not contractors.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they were working for the renderers, you see. But he was one fella, he was a... had there in the red rope. He had a contract with the company to supply him with so many men, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He would get people to pay him money for the job and achieve them? Yeah, how do you call that now?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was not children because he's not very good word, but...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was the middleman, that's for sure, but I don't know what...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, he was...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He sounds like he conned him a little bit. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Anyway, that's what they were doing. The colors that were here before you and they had a few. They understood some of the language. And they contracted. Of course, in the accident, that's what I'm working, you see here. They had an interpreter. They had a man next to the boss, you see. The interpreter, he wasn't working, he wasn't supposed to do common level. He was supposed to explain to the man what the boss had told him. And some of them interpreters, they didn't understand themselves. They should know what they did. They would tell them what to do.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because the fathers didn't work there. They just came from New York one day. They could speak the language. They had somebody to...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: To the boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came over, your father, he came over first, you sent him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, my father was here a couple of years before I was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I was still working out there in the bakery. And you find you send me a ticket, you know. Send me a ticket for me and my brother. And I was there. And we came here. He came here in Spokane in 1989.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think? What did you think when you saw this country?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I thought it was all right.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The country itself was all right. But working around it, I was dead of room. Because we were working hard them days. There was no money in the way. I was a young man from the bakery. When I came to pick and show over here, you see my hands were all blistered. And what I thought then, I thought if I had the money, I'd go right back. I did a job. But then after that, after a while, I got used to that, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And did pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get on?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: First you had to get, you came all the way to Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get off in New York?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, we had to get off in New York. And we, I still today, I don't remember what the DLE was there. But they put us in from one boat to the other. Then we went through the castle guard they called, they used to be, island in New York, where all the foreigners used to call me. I think they saw that island is, well anyway, we came through, in New York and they put us in another boat for 24 hours.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We traveled by boat. And then when we got out there, there was a lot of niggers, lots of niggers. It must have been on the south, because there were a lot of niggers. And the train was, well, they had to switch the cars in the side tracks, and they had to wait there for quite a while before they took us where we went up to town. And we found some of the Greek grocery stores, you know, that they had, they had grocery stores. And we bought some cheese and things, you know, from there to eat, because we were hungry, you see. When we left New York, they gave us a box of bologna and bread and so on. There are so many things, you know, over there. And we got right. Some day they needed that. But I did, I liked it too. Pie, one pie, you see, not pieced, but the whole pie. It was a box, pretty well, and we paid dollar for that box. That was lunch, box, but it was plenty of lunch there for 23 days. And we got fine. I mean, we got wrong pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then you came all the way by, did you come by train or not?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, by train, from there to, from there we come on the Northern Pacific Station in Spokane, from Burlington.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know where we, where we, the Burlington train, but there was a Burlington train from the east. And brought us over here to, to West, to Montana and so on.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And once in a while you see some of my Indians, you know, they used to come on the train, you know, we didn't know what they were. And they look funny, dressed in Indian costumes, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And we asked, of course, somebody there, and they say, see Indians, from,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: St. Louis, Missouri.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We met some of their fellows that were here before 23 years ago, and they could speak a few words English, some Greeks. And they, they knew that long time. They came to Spokane. They were shipping, they were shipping from there to, to Spokane through the railroad. They didn't pass, you see. They didn't have to pay. They did pass, and they were shipping, they were shipped from there to, to Spokane, and we got in Spokane. And it was fine and dandy with, with them, you see. Because if we want to eat something or something, you know, they always, they, they knew a few words and we didn't know any, you see. So that was pretty, pretty nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They got, they got to ship free because they worked on the railroad and they didn't work?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because they, they, they came here to work. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they wanted to send them to West, you see. The West was what they called Wild West them days. You see, they didn't, they didn't come over in West. The people didn't want to come to West, you see. They thought that the Eastern, Eastern towns were better. And of course, it wasn't bad when we came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this where most of the work was? Out West at that time?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, because there was all, you see, the Milwaukee was being a new road then. And it was coming to West, to Big Mountains, you know, and there was a lot of problems. It was handwork, you see. There's no machinery, you know, like it's today. It was all, all handwork. And it required a lot of men to, a lot of men to work to build a road. Now it is, it's different.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had to work with pick and shovel and whatever they had there and when they, if they work, or only against the mountain or something, they had to blast with a dynamite. And then they were off with, with the pick and shovel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you get on to work? Did you go to an employment agency or?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, no employment agency.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We had an interpreter and he went and got the job from the employment agency, of course. It was in Spokane, you know, there was a lot of, lot of employment agencies. And there was a lot of people working, I don't know how many, mostly foreigners. The Greeks, Italians, Austrians, all of them, you know, from all over the southern Europe.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It wasn't only Greeks, there were more Italians than anything else.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And Austrians, Austrians, they worked mostly in the mines.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they were working rather soon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you, where did you pick up this interpreter? Was he with you from, was he somebody you met in America?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, yeah, someone from the same country, you see. But he was here before us and he could speak a few words English, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Was he one of the guys you met in St.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Louis or somebody?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, it was here in the west.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When we came here, there were two or three of those fellows that were acting as interpreters.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And one of them would take a bunch and go out and rot. And yeah, they didn't do the same, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, they were, they were looking for a better job, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, if we went someplace and did nothing, why we quit?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because of ourselves.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But we very seldom did that because we run around, you know, you can, you know, my first job was in Walla Walla.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We were building a spore, a tractor to go into, to go into Kennedy, for their cannon fruits. To go into Kennedy and build a tractor to go into Kennedy because they didn't have no tractor. They had built a head canary and they were working. Everything was new, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And of course, they were building trucks in a lot of, like Kennedy's with wheat.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When, when, too low. We got something, we got something higher about that high.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You get a chance, uh, things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was the work organized? Did you have a little stretch that you did all by yourself or how did they do it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, they were all together.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The other job in railroad is, you don't know everything by yourself. You, uh, we were leveling the ground and of course you had to show all the dirt, the surplus dirt, you know, that was there and all the way on push cars. They had a car running by, running on a railroad, which they called push car and you're loading it, loading the dirt and wheel it where in place where they needed it, you know. No, you never work your own in railroad. It's, uh, you, you got to know when you, now you lay the tires down and then you lay the steel on the top of them. And of course you got to know the spark in them and everything. So the, uh, uh, uh, boxcars and so on, they, they can go on, you see. It was, uh, all, all together work that is, they, they didn't work alone by themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you make? What was the wages?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The wages, uh, the wages were a dollar and 30 cents a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right? How many hours was that for?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Ten hours. Ten hours.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some were a dollar and a half. Some were a dollar and a half. And of course there were jobs in different places where they were getting more, uh, say probably two dollars a day. Um, but there are contracts, you see, and uh, all of that, you, you have to, they already had taken the job.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: taken the... Yeah, well, the, uh, uh, other fellows. Other people, only a lot of people are, uh, the interpreters and so on and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard work?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I've heard... Hard work, sure, you see, it was hard work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was, uh, leopard by hand. Always it's hard work, you see. You, you, uh, then the train will come round, the work train will come round and, and throw gravel in the road and fill it, fill the inside and you, because there were dump cars, you see. And I saw some, some of the cars, they were dumping right in the middle of the trudge. Well, you had to show by hand off the trudge, which, which, you see, what was not needed, you see. It was alright if you had to raise the trudge high enough to put it under there. But, uh, what was not needed was you had to show off the... It was, uh, some places just up and down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you sleep?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yeah, we had, uh, uh, boxcars.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, well, not exactly boxcars, they were boxcars, but they were made for, for, uh, people to, to live on in the hair of windows and so on. Uh, and that's where we slept. We had to build our own, uh, our own beds and everything. In some, in those, they were already built. They were, uh, let's see, two, four, forty -eight, sixteen people in one boxcar. You see, they had bumps of, uh, of our one up and one down, you see. They had two, two layers.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Then, we, uh, uh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: all we wanted.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We had the boxcars called Moin. Uh, if we had to move four, five miles or ten miles or a hundred miles, uh, those boxcars would go with us. One time, I was a beggar. And I, uh, I backed the bread. And we dug, uh, holes in, uh, in the ground for oven. See, we burn, burn it with the tires, uh, all tires, you know. And, uh, I had, uh, I had just, uh, finished the bread and put it in the oven. It was about half done. And then, they come around, the, uh, the train come around and says, you moved to a certain place. We had to move to another place, you see, where, from the, uh, miles. So, so we had to get, we had to get the bread half, uh, half done.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because sometimes, you know, they, they can order to, they get the order to move. Sometimes, even nights, they come around, move you away from, uh, cause they don't move you away, they move you from the cars. You are in the, you are in the sleeping cars, where you have them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Must have known for the railroad, is it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, I'm interested in that. I am. And because I know a lot of men worked on that, and, and then worked.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The chief industry then was the railroad, you see. Because they are all new in the railroad, you see. They, they, they are not, uh, all in the middle, in Chicago, Milwaukee. They, uh, they are new. They come here west. At that time, 1907 is about a couple of years before I came. But they were still working up in Montana. They were pretty rough country over there, you see. They had to go through, through a rough country. It was a pretty expensive business. But, uh, they had the money, and, uh, they built them. And of course, they were, they wasn't paying very much. What, uh, about a dollar and a half, and two dollars a day, two dollars a day when I came here in, uh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: in, uh, in Badlach, 1914.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was everybody, uh, everybody working there where you were Greek? Was it all, all from the same place? Working on that railroad?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: All, uh, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, there were bunches, you see. Now, one interpreter probably will have, uh, thirty men, or forty, another interpreter. Uh, he probably, uh, will have thirty Italians or thirty different, uh, different nationalities. But they were separate. Sometimes they, they get together and work together, but, uh, most of the times, they separate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the men that you worked with, men that you come over with from Greece?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, men that I knew. I either come over with them, or they were here before, before me.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: More so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many from your own, from your own hometown?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, from my hometown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You started out together then?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You, you came at the same time from... Ah, well, not the same time, not at all. Some were here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some of the fellows were here, you know, before they were here, maybe, two, three years. Two years anyway. But, uh, when we went out to work, we, uh, never already working sometimes, some of them. And we, uh, we didn't have to buy a ticket them days because you, you get a pass, you see, and, uh, send you into, to the, to the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you met up with some of these guys, uh, when you were working?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, and I knew so long. I knew I must, all of them, because they were from the same town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the crew, was the crew, did, did they work as, as one group?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You said that if you didn't like it, you could quit and go someplace else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be the whole crew would decide if they didn't want it? Did that happen to you?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. Once. Or twice.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We went to, uh, the job out of Coeur d 'Alene, I think it was. And we didn't, they didn't, they didn't have nothing there, you see, the facilities of any kind. And, uh, they had tents to live in. So we delighted and the whole crew went, went back and we found different, different job. There we were.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In employment office, you see, you, you're not the, not the job. Sometimes you don't get a job, you probably have to wait two, three days or a week. And then, something will turn up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you never walked off the job when you were working?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, no, no, we don't, we didn't do that. We just went to one place that I remember and then they have nothing there, you see. So we, we had to quit. And they, it was, it was a railroad. It was a contract, you see. Some of the, they were, they were making a great railroad. But it was away from, from the railroad and we didn't have no, and, uh, they, uh, the office over there and, and they left. So we had to hire some farmers, you know, the workers to all our belongings and we walked. I think it was, uh, about ten miles we walked that time. From, uh, from the work to the, uh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I know the name of the place now, I can't think of.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was a town, into the town.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was close, close in Spokane.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well some, some, uh, not my guy, it's another, another little town there. I went and got it, got it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was an electric car that took us Spokane.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Someone that I, I talked to who, who also worked on the section told me that it was slavery. That's what he called it. Well, it was, it was hard work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You had to work ten hours, you know. And it was, uh, it was hard work with, uh, you did use it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, he didn't call it slavery because you could quit anytime you want to. But, uh, slavery is something that you can avoid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you save money from that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, not very much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The things were, uh, yeah, off -achievement days, but, uh, the living was cheap. Then we, uh, we had some older fellas, you know, that, uh, they knew how to save money. And, uh, we saved our way a few dollars, but not very much money. It wasn't, uh,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: or, we lived, uh, ten or fifteen dollars a month. And, uh, we were getting about $30 or $35.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: $35 because it's a dollar and a half, dollar and a half, dollar and a half, $0 .35 with one dime. And that wasn't very much, but, uh, they raised that to a dollar and a half. And we were making about $30, uh, $35 a month, or $40. And we saved a little bit, but not very much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some of the old fellas, you know, that, uh, here before, why they, uh, they knew how to manage life and save.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, the section is different, uh, in the section you, you are worked on section two, but, uh, in the section you, you don't have too much to do in winter time. Uh, because the trach is already, uh, surfaced during the summer.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And, uh, in, uh, winter time we didn't have too much to do, but in the summer you work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They have, you know, they have to raise low spots and so on.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's more maintenance than construction. Yes, well, yes, maintenance. It was maintenance crew.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's what the section is, maintenance crew. But they have to, they have to work. It's, uh, they always find the work to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you meet up with your father when you were working on a railroad?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes. He was working with you too. Yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At one time I left when my cousin went down to, who are you going to work in a dam?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They're building dam, you know, and they're paying more wages, of course. And, uh, that white salmon, you know, also, uh, white salmon, also in the Oregon.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Washington is on, uh, on the other side.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Cross river.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They're building a pipe line. You know, they're putting a big, pretty big pipe for water to go through and, uh, powerhouses. For powerhouses, you know, they're building a dam. I don't remember now what it, uh, looked like.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But there was a way of building who you were working on, uh, cements, you see, where they were damming the water.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Then, of course, they dam one side of the river and then they throw the water on the other side and then they, you know, they, it's, uh, yeah, they were paying more wages. It was hard work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I thought I could work there. It was too hard. So I didn't have to stay with him all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the guys who worked there, uh, on the railroad, were they, did they go into town and, and blow in like the lumberjacks did?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not so much. Some, yeah, but, uh, they don't, um, they didn't do it because they didn't have much to blow in. And they had to work all the time. Winter and summer. Lumberjacks, of course, they, uh, they were laid off. They didn't work on camps during the winter months. They work in summer. They make pretty good money. They were in the zippo, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And, uh, they were making pretty good money, but, um, they had, uh, spent it during the winter months.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's different in the lumbering and different in the, uh, railroad because you, you can work in a railroad doing the, the winter months in some places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, did you work all year round?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, mostly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you guys weren't working, would you go back to Spokane? When we're not working?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, yeah, we went to Spokane for another job. Therefore we didn't have one. But we usually stayed in the job. I was, uh, the first year I worked in railroad, uh, they laid off the, uh, then, and they kept, they kept four, four men in two sections. You see, there was two sections, two, there was double section in, uh, Efrada. And the other one was, uh, going east from Efrada. And they kept us, they're all winners. We didn't do nothing. But we, uh, we had to watch and watch the, uh, well, as you men in the school, you see, if there's something you do while you, we do it. And if it wasn't, we, uh, we wasn't, we wasn't doing anything then. Uh, because there was nothing to do in wintertime. And that trash was all fixed in, uh, during the summer months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have heat in the, in the cars, in the boxcars? Was there heat in the winter?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had wood stoves?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But barely, you know, wood stoves. And in the section, of course they had a section of ours. You see, and, uh, we had heat over there, too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They, uh, phone used to call. We wouldn't call in some, some places. And they, company phone used to call. Or we, uh, sometimes if we, uh, if there's a, uh, car with a call, we, we go up there and, uh, throw some of it off anyhow. They wanted it or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you work for a contractor or for the railroad directly?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh, for the railroad directly. Uh, these, uh, interpreters, there were no contractors. They were just working for wedges. They were probably paying them a little more because they knew the language. But, uh, they had no contract.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There were no contractors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a, there was a, a, a, and they were, you had to, you had no boss then among yourselves, except for the interpreter?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was the only. Oh, no, no. We had the boss, uh, American man. Oh. Sure. Oh, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Sometimes two bosses in a big, um, big game.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: One, uh, was, uh, one head foreman, one assistant foreman.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they treat you well? The, the, the bosses? Or did, or did they look down on you because you couldn't speak English?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, they, uh, they looked down upon you and you, you were, you were just forced to work, that's all there was to it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And of course I, uh, I was young man, I didn't care in days, you know. I, I could work. They never bothered me, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Young and strong. Strong back in the week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What you want?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Mind.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, you had to have a strong back them days. Now I don't think I can do anything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Now are you talking off, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About the section?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: About the railroad, I mean. About the railroad. Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Let's talk about butlers.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, okay. Let's talk about potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you, when did you come now? How did you happen to come, come to potlatch? You know where I came from? You know, where happiness, happiness junction is? In, in Oregon?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I was working in the railroad. That's happiness.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they lay us off. I and my brother. And we had two cousins here, two years to communicate with them. And they brought us to come over here. They were here before, they were here, uh, maybe two, three years before we came here. Yeah, they were here two years. They came here in 1912.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In the middle.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So we came here. And I got a job...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In, uh, Slasher.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was a... All four men there were... He asked me if I ever worked in the sawmill. I told him yes. I lied.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well... Down in Oregon. And I never did work in the sawmill but I told him yes. So he told me to go ahead and... Work in certain job. And in that job where he told me... He, well, he was a geek. Working there and he motioned to me to...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: To come over.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So I found a place without knowing. I didn't know what, what is Slasher meant. Or what was... What he, what he called a place, you see. And I got in there and the fella that worked there, he was off. Or he was going, he quit. I guess he quit because he didn't come back.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Anyway...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I got a job there. And I'm still here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that fella teach you how to do the job? The Greek fella?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, it wasn't much to that job. It was semi...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Uh... Sudden scale, you see. It wasn't scale work. Anybody could do it. It was strange slaps, you see. To cut them four feet. But if you don't cut them, it was alright too. Cut them for life. They were making life in days.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Now they don't make life. They use other materials. At that time they were making life. And there were a lot of men working in the... in the last mill. I don't know how many, but there were quite a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of this, of working in Potlatch compared to working on the railroad?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, I thought it was alright.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's why I stayed.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I liked it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I liked it here because for one thing, you don't have the train to come over there and pick up the... pick up the cars and move you probably 100 miles or 200 miles or 50 miles. And you didn't have the slides. Sometimes they had a slide in the...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Wenatchee and up there in those places, they had a slide one time there and we had to go in the mud.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not thick.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they took us from the section. They picked us up. They didn't tell us what we were going and what we were going.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And we...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We were over there all night and all day the next day and we never did finish the jump. The railroad was pretty rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was waist -deep mud you were working in?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In a railroad, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who knows what the railroad was? What were you supposed to be doing? Shoveling the mud out?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, shoveling everything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You see, you were supposed to... they had scrapers. But in some places, they couldn't use it. And you had to shovel mud with the shovel.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that's why I liked the potlatch. The middle was all right. Because I did Wenatchee. We had the houses here, you know, and we... Not here, but down below we had different houses. They were temporary houses, they called them. They had built them temporary for the people that came here. And they found that they could rent them to us and Italians.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they kept them during World War II, they sold them. Of course, that was late then, you know, that's when they sold the whole business here in town.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But you came here by yourself, not...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: or did you come with a bunch of guys?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, by myself and my brother. There were two of us.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: My brother didn't get out here, so he went to work on the section. Over here, there's a section over here. I think they still got a section over here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But there was already a lot of Greek people here at the time. Oh, yeah, for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how many?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There were about 30 people from my hometown alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, there were other people. Yeah, they were on two shifts, you see. They were on two shifts and there was lots of them. Lots of work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your hometown?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They all worked in the middle, in the sawmill. All the Greeks worked in the sawmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your hometown again?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I didn't ask you that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the town?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Didimau. D -D -Mau.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: M -A -H. That's where they spell it, but it's not the right name.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The Greek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come so many people just from your own hometown came here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There were so many out here in the West, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No one came out here in the West. There were very few left in the East. You see, one came, then the other came, and then they had correspondence, you see. And they said, you come over here and you did a job. That's the way I came here. See, I had cousins here. I had two cousins here. In fact, there was three cousins. One is still living, two of them died.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I came over here and got a job where he was living down below. Here I told you about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well, how was it set up?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That place there was set up. It was a long, long building. It was divided into two rooms. One room, no, it had two bedrooms and a kitchen and dining room together.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There were four of us living in that place. And in other places, they were living probably more than four because Doblop slept together, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a long building, like a dormitory?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, a long building, but it was divided into small apartments, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In the war, there was no war in it. The war was only outside. You had to get it from the pipe.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was a pipe over there, all right?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The toilets were the same way. They were separated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the toilets like the ones up here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, well, yes, like the ones up here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Over there, they were separated. They were at the house that place was long. And then they had separate buildings for each place for a toilet. You see, they had small toilets for each one of those places. And of course, here they are here in town, here in town where there was no up in Nappil, they had modern houses. But downtown here, they never had a box over there. You go in there and you do your stuff. And then once a week, I think, or twice a week, no, once a week or once a month, they had men. They come and empty them in a wagon and haul them away. They were holding them to a Japanese farm up here, and he was raising the best stuff.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He used it to manure his vegetables.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He didn't want the place. It was common. Everything was common. You could want nothing.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But when you lived there, there was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many all told, would you say, were Greeks there in those buildings?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: 25 or 30. And there was not only Greeks there, but the building was pretty long. It was the one, it was two buildings. But the two were the longest.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It started fire, you see. I told you how it started fire and it burned down.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Tell me that again, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It got fire. One of the fathers there, the Greeks, he was a Greek, too. He was from the old country. He didn't know anything about fire. Then he took the ashes from the stove and he put them in a box. He put them in a box and left them there, you see. Pretty soon, about four o 'clock in the morning, the masses cut fire. Building the floor and the whole business was on fire. They had enough time to throw their stoves out, the belongings. All the number was hurt. They burned down the whole business because there was no water, no hydrogen there. They didn't have no hydrogen. That was just a very building, you see, for the foreigners.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That was what was left, why they tore it out anyway. They shouldn't have that in the first place, but they did. They brought us up here in town, in different houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it happen?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had housing available up here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They did. It happened to have empty houses, so I don't know why. Because the foreman, now they're a middle foreman, we didn't go down, they didn't work. They had to shut the mill down there. He says, take them up there. There's lots of houses, large empty houses up there. They brought us down to this street, not here, not here in this street. Down in the other street, they were warm, they were temporary houses, those two temporary houses too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They used them because they found a place to, they found that they could rent them to foreigners. Those houses down there, they were not like this here. They were temporary buildings, you know, not the blue river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say they had to shut the mill down because you guys couldn't go to work?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, we couldn't go to work. They had to hold it down for a while in the morning because we didn't go down there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And then of course they told them that they could get the houses up there. They wanted to work in the evening, they were working ten hours. You see in the evening they come up and got the houses here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the people just waiting to find out what they were going to do?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't have any place to... Yeah, naturally, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't know what was going to do. And the boss told them that they'll have houses up there for you. And you can move up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there about as many Italians as Greeks there then?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: More Italians. And Swedes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But the Swedes are all working yard, piling lumber. And the Italians too, the Italians work in the green chain. The more Italians there are 32 Italians working in the green chain alone. And other works, you know, other places here and there, you know, there's probably 50 Italians there you are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how does it work out that the Greeks worked in the sawmill and the Italians worked on the green chain?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's where they started.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They started there and one by one you see there, they were all over there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's why.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's where they started to see. And one by one you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: All the common labor and semi -skier labor was done by the Greeks in the middle. And it was a lock mill there too, you know. There's a lot of work in the lock mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of the people though, they never had a sawmill back in Greece had they?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. They had a sawmill.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they, you know, they put it on, you know. So it's a matter of time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the difference between semi -skilled and skilled work in the sawmill?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well there is a difference. Now it's nothing. It's all skill. All semi -skilled. But them days, the difference is that they had a tensile. Well, not skier work. That's a common labor. And they had a set of, not just skier work.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You got to know how to do it. You can get up there and do that work. And my job was semi -skilled. So you could put anybody up there in a cage and tell him to pull saws. And because you don't know what to pull and what to leave, that was semi -skilled. And the following days, I was throwing the lumbar on the top of the teamer. It was a common labor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you do when you got into the mill, mostly?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When saws on the teamer.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I was training lumbar in other words. And you want to know what to trim, what to cut up, what to cut up and what to let go. You see, you got to, and yet, other pieces of lumbar are the best part. And if it's to be cut in two, you do not. But you have to understand. You almost have to be a great do not, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That sounds pretty skilled, skilled to me.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes, it is if you do it right.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That is working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that's funny?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Ask me questions because I can go ahead and tell you. Okay.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I wonder is how close, how close did you stick with the people that you knew from your hometown?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: How close I stayed.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, all the time. While they were here. Up to, I had one year. I was left here, you see, same as I. And he left here in 19,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: 40, 45, I think, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, no, no. And I was with him because we spoke, we always spoke in our own language.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Unless someone would come here other than Greek, you know, and we have to speak English.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And it's one, there's some time how I learned to speak the English language as well. Because down in Somiel, you can't talk, you have to use signs. You talk sign language in Somiel. Because it's too noisy there, you can't talk.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And over here, when I came home, I spoke Greek.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And sometimes I'm wondering how did I learn to speak English so well. I don't know how I do speak well now, I don't, because it can be a gift to all. And my mind is not steady.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You speak good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You speak good, do you know, do you have any idea how you learn to speak?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: What you say, you... It's one thing to do, one thing to do, something that you really want to do, because in order to stay here in America, you have to be able to speak English. And I was trying to learn as much as I could. And we went to school, night school, in 1915,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But that didn't last very long, you see, we...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: World War I started, and I moved to... our coast. I went for the shipyards there, for 18 months I think. Then I came back here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think, did you leave because the pay would be better over there?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I thought yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was better. But it was more expensive to leave there anyway. So it didn't gain very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like it better here than on the coast, as a place to live?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And of course, my cousins were here then too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That made a difference.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In effect, one of my cousins was down to Portland, and when I was laid off in the yards on the whole stop, I came here with him. And of course, there was a lot of other groups here too then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get much opportunity when you were here to speak English? If you spent most of your time with your friends and they all spoke Greek?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I think you didn't get any opportunity, but in 1945, I joined the Masonic Lodge.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I had to learn some other work there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But at that time, there wasn't any Greeks. There was only one or two, I think, here. And we left shortly after that. And that's what I induced me to learn to speak because I had to. I took interest in work, you know, in Masonic work, I mean.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I liked it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So I had steady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So does that mean that in the early years, like, oh, from when you first came into the 20s, that you didn't speak much English and your other friends didn't either?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, in 20s, I think I spoke with you fairly good.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, probably not. But from the beginning, I had the willing to learn as much as I could from the language.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I tried to master the English language, but found it impossible.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Because now they tell me its expression and you say that it's all Greek to me. Well, the Greek language, it may be hard, but the English is hard. Because you have so many words that are,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: the Homan names, you call them, they are about the same, but they spell different. The spelling in the English language is the hardest thing of all. I can spell it now, even. And you have words that are letters that are silent. You have many, many, many letters that are silent. You see, you have to learn to speak them without hesitating at all.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In the Greek language, you don't have no silent letters. You have to sound every one of them. The only difference is not in the Greek language. When you use two letters together, you call them...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a diphthong?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Diphthongs. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you, when you first came, were you planning on going back?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. Did you ever plan on going back after, well, you said at the very beginning you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wanted to?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, yeah, in the very beginning, because I came from the back, it is soft hands, you see. And I got all the hands. The work was hard, you see, then. Because I was used to it, I was too soft for picking shoulder. And that's why I didn't like them. After I got used to that, it wasn't hard. It was all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they leave at the beginning of the Depression?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was nothing here. They shut the mill down. The mill was down here for two years during the Depression. And I left here too. I would not lose them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They couldn't get any way to support themselves?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No. I thought the company was, you know, trying to get work for people.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, to get work for people that had families. They had to take care of them, whether they wanted or not. You see, there were a lot of people here that were caught here. They stayed here. They gave them, probably, one week. About to work one week and one week to lay off. You see, there were a lot of them that were in a hole here. The company had to get the money. They were all in store. And the rent was a rent. They didn't care, you see, but the store is very different. But they did a lot of work. When we had never paid for the rent at all, they didn't have the money during the Depression. They had to keep the fellows with the families. Of course, they let us off. There was only one Greek that was working down near the planner. And because he was an old -timer, they didn't lay him off, but they cut him down. They let him work for one day a week, sometime. Sometime less.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: One day a month.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was kicking, but others didn't have any job at all.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, you'd worked for 20 years by then. Yes, I had worked 20 years. I was down in the soil, Mr. O 'Connor.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: O 'Connor was a general manager here at that time. You didn't know him, I think. And he told me, yes, we want you. He says, we all want you to go to work, because we know that some of the fellows that have families are no good. They can't do the job that you do. But still, we have to... We're looking at the thing in the human side. It was humanity that they hired them because of their children. If it wasn't for their children, they would have been now.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But, of course, that happened. As I told you, that fella there that was working in a replan, he wanted to work on a section and let me have the job.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that's how I got on. There was one family in a ruckric, two single men were working in the middle. All the others were men with problems.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was this when the depression was ending or still during?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It was ending. Then you came back and took his job.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, I gave it to him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But by then, most of the Greeks were gone and didn't come back. No, there was nothing for them to come back. When the mill started up again, they didn't hire back too many people.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not very many, no. Because they were all here, you see. When the mill started here, they run probably six, seven months a year. Then they shut down in wintertime for two, three years up to 1940. Then they started up. Of course, then World War II come up. Then it was a long period.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Would you tell me again about your meetings with Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Laird?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, there is nothing I can tell you, but Mr. Laird wasn't...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you thinking of aristocrat?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, aristocrat. That's right.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And it's a Greek word, but I can't hear it. Yeah, he was aristocrat. There was one old fellow down there, and his brother -in -law was killed in the woods. He was in a fire, a forest fire. And this fellow had little money tied up in his knee in a handkerchief. And the old man wanted to get that money to go back to the old country himself, because he was too old to work here. So he asked me to go down with him and talk to Mr. Laird. And his office then was down the plant. They didn't have this office here. They didn't have this building here. So I went down. I said, I can go down with you. I said, but I don't know where I can make him understand.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So I went down there, and he took the money in the handkerchief and showed to me, and he said that will go to his widow, because she's entitled to this.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I don't know where I said anything else, because I told him that this man is his brother -in -law, and he wants to find out what happened to the money that he had, because he knew that he had some money, you see. And that's why he took it out of the yard there and showed. He says this money is run to the widow.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he says after the things are strained out here, he says we send it to the widow,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: and he can't get anything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: So I left.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I didn't even know what the widow was then, you see. So I left there, and of course the old man, he sent a guy, a fellow from Spokane, and he was married to his sister. And his sister, this fellow that died was married to his sister, and had his sister to give him the power of attorney. And that way he got money and whatever else was worth it for him, for the death. Always wanted the widow for this death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it didn't go to the guy's widow?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, because she gave him power of attorney, this old fellow, to her brother. Anyway, this was her brother, you see. And of course he told her that that was the best way and the easiest way to get the money is to give him power of attorney and to go ahead and settle the cash with him. And he did. Another time that I talked to him, I'd be talking a little more English then, but it was up here. And I had to go over there and ask the secretary. The secretary was downstairs, and she had a window. That window is still there now, but it's for different purposes. She had a window, and I says, Miss Kelly, can I see Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lert?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You had to ask the secretary for permission to go and see him. And Miss Kelly, she called him on the telephone. She says a Greek fella, and I guess she told him, because she knew I was a Greek. I was here for a long time then, you know. And yes, she says, I'll send him up. So there were two of them, of course. He, myself and the fella that had broke his leg. And I told him, Mr. Lert says this man had an accident in the middle and broke his leg. And he's been laid off now for quite a while, and he'd like to have, he'd like to know if he can get some money to send to his family.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he told me then that he, he says you won't tell him. That we have nothing to do, that now the state is taking care of that. And he'd be compensated from the state.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Only time is ripe. He had to go through the state to get his money. But he says if he has family and his family needs money, anyway he has some friends he can borrow from his friends and send to his family.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And that's the only thing we can do for him. So I got up and left. And I guess he, I think, I don't remember now, but I think he got some money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't know how much. Well, what was Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lert like to talk to? What did you think to talk to him?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, he was a gentleman. He was a aristocrat, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He spoke to me pretty nice, because I could talk to him in English. But he wasn't, you know, he wasn't hard to boil or anything. Some people probably had different impressions. But I think he was a nice fellow to talk to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he cared a lot about the town and the people? That he took a lot of care about that?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I think he did, yes. I think he did.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I think he was a humanitarian. He was not, you know, a rough customer and didn't care for people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were people afraid of him, do you think? Especially the people who didn't know the town?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was not too good. They were not afraid of him.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't think. I don't think they were afraid of him. There was one occasion here where the bank was owned by the company.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the banker emblazed $250 ,000 from the bank. And all the Greeks here, you know, they got scared. And Russia was there in the bank to take the money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And there went over there himself, or he sent somebody else to tell the people that they're not going to lose their money. The company is going to pay them every sense. I think he sent, followed by the name, no. That's right. He himself went down there and told him that the boys, you can throw your money if you want to, but don't be afraid because you're not going to lose your money. The company is going to pay, and he did. They pay everything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And of course they put that fellow in jail. That's when the examiner came over, and he stayed here for quite a while. Followed by the name of Warren and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were back in the early days when all the Greeks were living together pretty much, what was your social life like? What kinds of things did you guys do?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, our social life here was among us. We gambled so much, but not very much here in Padlac. All the social life between us and the other people, we lived apart.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: We didn't have too much to do because during the war, there were some young fellows who went down there. I was young too then, but I didn't go. They went down to the gymnasium. They had a gymnasium here, and went down there to join. You had to pay so much while you were small, to be a member, and they would learn. But as far as our social life here, we have among ourselves, and we just... Sometimes we had somebody from the town come down here. But not very often. When we were all Greeks, you know, I mean...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Then we didn't have much social life. We played cards very much, first time.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Was that...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Pinacao.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did guys drink very much?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some. Some did.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I tired of it very much.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had...

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Over there, where we... I was never to them. I was living right next door.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the other houses, you know, were all like onions. In case they had wooden balls, and they were playing with them. It's something like a bowling alley, but it was open.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had a little ball. They played that in all countries, you know. In my country, too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But here they were speaking Italian, and I couldn't understand them. Pretty soon I probably found to understand some words. But that's their social life every time that the sun is, and every time they're in the evenings, in summertime, when the nights are... I mean, when the days are long, they were playing them balls all the time. It was kind of funny to ask them, and sometimes we play too, but not very often. Because they owned the balls, you see. They had bought them, I guess, or brought them back from the old country. I don't know why, but then when they had... Then they drank, too.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I heard a lot of wine came in.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, well, these fellows there, they played for the drinks, you see. And there was a fellow there that was a good lady, and the company didn't know anything like that. He had it all, but it was private, you know. They didn't bother us anyway. And they drank vermit. He was quite a drinker, one of them. He was drunk, you know, all the time.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he was drunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have the Italians, did they have one man who represented them, their interest to the company?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, they had one man that was a former, was boss, down at Green Chain. And he had about 30, 35 men, I think. But he didn't represent them. He thought he did himself, but he didn't actually represent them. He was trying to make himself known to the authorities here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But I don't think he represented them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was brown -nosing, yeah?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, and another thing, they went on a trudge one time.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And he did cause a trudge, he told me.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And I think the company gave me some, they settled up anyway. Because they went on a trudge without notifying the company, you know. And the mail stopped. What happened here, the Green Chain is on a trudge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is out there? Reno?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, she was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was married, man. He had two daughters, one of them still living, he's down there.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: He was a nice fellow, he was doing it too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're saying he kept working when the men went on strike?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no, he was. He was the cause of the strike. He was the one that told, I think, I don't know for sure now, but I think that's what he was. He's doing it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many people sending part of their earnings back to the old country?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some, some were.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Some daughters that they had families, you know. And of course they had some, to support their families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they tell you about the shares they had here in the store? I've heard them mention, they were pretty big, huh?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, this here was the biggest store around the Inland Empire, outside Spokane. Because they were supporting only, it was an outlet for the camps, you know. And they had, and there was a lot of people here, there was probably a thousand people working here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they had, they had free coffee, and there were fathers from, there were no automobiles in there. It was wagons, and they were coming from palos, from all around these small towns. They were coming here to the cell. They had a big, oh, that was a big, big, big doings.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And they were successful, I think they were making money.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But this store was pretty, pretty well supplied, you see, they had everything. Even from farming, they were coming in all, from distance, with the wagons and the boggies, whatever you call them,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: from all over.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I think they were once a year, the cells, during the fall.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The man that was the store manager, I forget his name. It was McDonough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, he was a real Wheeler dealer, wasn't he?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: McDonough, he was a businessman. And after he left, why even, everything went haywire. Oh, not haywire, but they didn't have themselves. But themselves were, oh, they were big, big doings. Yeah, and free coffee, and everything.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But they are coming, people come from all over.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Do you think back then that you spoke more, if you were the guy that went in with some of your friends, people that were opposed to immigration then, and I think that probably there was, from what I read, they say there was prejudice against these new people coming in, because they were changing their attitude about immigrants. Yeah, well, there wasn't much, there wasn't very many immigrants coming in from 1920,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: 1910, yes, a lot of them, but not in 20s. From 1910 to 1915, probably, well, 1920 some, but not too many. Not too many immigrants, you know, they put that, a quote on, and there's so many, they can only come so many from each.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, I think the quota was a part of that deal, because they were trying, wanting to stop people from coming in.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, they did, they did stop them. There's only a hundred people coming from the police, and that quota is full all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, a hundred people a year? Do you think that, do you remember there being prejudice against the people when they first came over here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't mean just Potlatch, but out west.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, no, because they needed them, then, they needed them to build a country, and without them it wouldn't have been built. But, there, it might have been prejudice,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: that everything was, well, like now, everything is built, you see, you get a bunch of foreigners, like this, for instance, as refugees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From Vietnam?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yes. Because, you know, it was just, I think, a lot of people resented.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: In June, I think, I don't know, but this,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: because you have to give them something to do, you have to give them a job to support themselves and foreigners if they have them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They have foreigners too, don't they?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But, like, you say, for instance, here, they didn't want, they couldn't be in the gym at first, and I heard that the Italians, they didn't, they weren't made to feel welcome at the Catholic Church at first, when they first tried to go there, so they didn't go for a while.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Yeah, well, I'll tell you, the Catholic Church, it's, the Italians that were here were very few that went to church, not because they didn't want them, because they didn't want to call themselves.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And the church, the Catholic Church, I don't know what they're, I think, what they believe in, but at one time, that father there, that nousio that I was talking to you, telling you, about being the foreman of the Grinch and being a drinker, he never did call church. And when he died, they never took him to church.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And these Italians that were here, you know, they were, they were men, but I don't think they could do anything. And I asked him, I'm not an Italian, but I knew that this father here, and I know the Greek church, the Greek Orthodox Church is the same, too, but, and they were, they were very good. They never do that. They take him in church anyway.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: You mean it was the, the town didn't do it. The church didn't do it.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, it was the, it was the Catholic priest. I asked him, he says, why didn't you, he says, I had orders from the bishop, from boys. Whether that was right or not, I don't know. The bishop didn't know anything about this man.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Nothing was him. He was a young fella.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, did the, was there practice of the Greek Orthodox here? Did the men have any services here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, it's a church in Moscow, but not here. Not here, nowhere else around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you couldn't, or you didn't get together among yourselves to have any kind of worship?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They were priests. There was a priest come here one time, and he stayed here for some time. But two, three days, I think, and he had services here. But that's a long time ago, and they were leaving him back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any way then that the men could worship? I mean, did they do it on their own, or just have to forget about it?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Well, they do it on their own, if they want.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: It's up to the individual.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: The church is alright. If you want to go to church, it's okay. And if you don't, you don't have to go to church to be here with a Christian.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I go to church here. I used to, I don't go anymore because I got to all, but I couldn't go to church in a Greek church, so I got the Presbyterian here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the early days, did other Greeks go to the churches in town here, or did they just not?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: No, no other Greeks, no.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: That's when they left. After the Greeks left, you see, since the depression that the Greeks left here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And those two, three that were here, they go to church once in a while. I used to go quite often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most of the, well, how do you think that most of the Greeks felt about being here? Do you think most of them liked to work and were satisfied with it or wanted something better?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Oh yeah, they were satisfied. That's why they stayed here. Those, you know, that were here.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't like something better. Well, they didn't like something better, but they weren't in position to get anything better, you see.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: And some, not very many, not left here. They went into business for themselves or did something else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think many people believed in the idea of getting, of being able to get real rich here?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There wasn't much change. You can't get rich by working.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Now, if this was open town,

speaker: Gus Demus
words: from the beginning, there would have been a lot of Greeks in business.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Not Larram, but there had been some, like in Elk River there was one. In Elk River, a very small place.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: There was.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: But here it's a big, bigger place, you see, and there would have been some. Being a communist town, not permit anything, intoxicated liquor.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't go into business this year.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They had business here, of course. The confectionary was run by Americans. Then they made monosome. But it was straight business, you know.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: They didn't sell no beer because it was permitted by the company, and it was a communist town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's any difference of how well people could do in a company town as compared to a town, or here as compared to Elk River? Was this a better place or a worse place?

speaker: Gus Demus
words: This was a good place if it was open like Yom Kivel, but it wasn't. I mean, difference.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: I don't mean just for business. I'm thinking of working in the mill. Working in the mill is not much difference.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: Working in the mill you have the same thing in here, and yes, you have in Elk River.

speaker: Gus Demus
words: When the mill was run over there.
